query: Hormone replacement therapy and prevention of coronary artery disease

retrieved_docs.title: Hormone Replacement Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Hormone Replacement Therapy and the Heart
retrieved_docs.content: In the WHI trial's CEE/MPA arm, the overall incidence of coronary heart disease increased by 18% compared to the placebo group, though this increase was not statistically significant. After the intervention ended, the risk of cardiovascular disease returned to baseline levels. In the CEE-only trial, coronary heart disease risk decreased by 6% in the treatment group, with myocardial infarction rates decreasing by 3%, but these reductions were also not statistically significant. A more favorable interaction was observed for myocardial infarction in women aged 50 to 59 compared to older women, and this benefit persisted after the intervention. Therefore, the "timing hypothesis" was suggested in which early HRT at the time of menopause for only 10 years or less and not starting HRT in those older than 60 was recommended. This hypothesis has since been refuted. Additionally, 7.4 years after treatment, women who received estrogen therapy alone had significantly lower coronary artery calcium levels. The cardiovascular risks returned to baseline at a cumulative follow-up of 13 years. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone: In 2014, the FDA released a required labeling change for low testosterone products for the use of low testosterone due to aging due to a possible increased risk of heart attack and stroke. Subsequent studies and reviews have not been consistent in this correlation. Some reviews have even indicated a potential beneficial cardiovascular effect when treating low testosterone with testosterone supplementation. Further study is needed to provide more clarity on this specific topic. [68] [69] [70] [71] [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Hormone Replacement Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Women's Health Initiative Trial
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, the 2002 WHI trial reported that menopausal estrogen-progesterone therapy increased the risk of breast cancer, stroke, and coronary heart disease in women. These findings were widely publicized, contributing to the fear of HRT, despite most negative outcomes losing significance when adjusted with multiple statistical analyses, as per the review by Hodis and Sarrel published in 2018. [32] Hodis and Sarrel reported that the unusually low breast cancer incidence in the placebo group was what skewed the outcomes, making the results controversial. Clinicians should also remember that the WHI trial patients were not randomized according to baseline breast cancer risk, as this was not intended to be a breast cancer risk trial. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Menopause -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Hormonal Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic HRT's effects on cardiovascular disease in postmenopausal women vary based on when the treatment is started. Beginning HRT within 10 years of the final menstrual period has been shown to decrease cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality. However, the use of hormones is not recommended solely for preventing heart disease. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Hormone Replacement Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Hormone Replacement Therapy and the Heart
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast to CEE, endogenous estradiol is known to be cardioprotective. The ELITE (Early Versus Late Postmenopausal Treatment With Estradiol) was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial that found a benefit to cardiac health with estradiol. This study suggests that the cardiovascular risks identified in the WHI trial may be specific to conjugated equine estrogen (CEE) and are not linked to transdermal estradiol or the timing of estrogen replacement therapy, as was previously believed. [5] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Postmenopausal women have reduced estrogen, which increases the likelihood of developing atherosclerosis and CAC. Estrogen is thought to protect the vascular system by promoting endothelial function and reducing oxidative stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Menopause (Nursing) -- Medical Management -- Hormonal Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Hormone therapy can treat vasomotor symptoms and prevent vaginal/urogenital atrophy, as well as preserve an advantageous lipoprotein profile and prevent bone loss. It can be given in various forms (i.e., tablets, creams, patches), in different modalities (i.e., continuous versus cyclic), and is available as systemic estrogen, estrogen-progestin, estrogen-bazedoxifene, progestin alone, or combined oral contraceptives. The use of unopposed estrogen may cause uterine hyperplasia and uterine cancer, therefore, should be avoided in women with a uterus. The cyclical administration of combination estrogen-progestin therapy is recommended for women with an intact uterus.  It significantly decreases the severity and frequency of hot flashes and improves urogenital atrophy and sleep disturbances. It is also useful in preventing osteoporosis and associated fractures. However, hormone therapy should only be used for the shortest duration of time and at its lowest effective dose, as it increases the relative risk of breast cancer, ovarian cancer, thromboembolism, stroke, and coronary heart disease. There is an increased breast cancer risk after 3 to 5 years with estrogen-progestin and 7 years with estrogen only. It is contraindicated in those with a history of breast cancer, endometrial cancer, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, liver disease, unexplained vaginal bleeding, and coronary heart disease. For atrophic vaginitis, in particular, systemic or vaginal estrogen can be utilized; however, localized estrogen therapy at very low doses is preferable when there are no other systemic symptoms. The use of localized estrogen therapy (via vaginal rings, creams, or tablets) has been shown to enhance blood flow and reverse vaginal atrophy. However, this also carries a small risk of venous thromboembolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Menopause -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Hormonal Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: It is unclear if systemic HRT influences the risk of coronary heart disease, as more studies need to be done in this area. Estrogen plus progesterone therapy increases the risk of breast cancer, stroke, pulmonary embolism, and deep vein thrombosis but not coronary heart disease. Estrogen is contraindicated in those with a history of breast cancer, endometrial cancer, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, liver disease, and unexplained vaginal bleeding. Although they are sometimes used off-label, levonorgestrel-containing intrauterine devices are not FDA-approved for protecting the endometrium. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tamoxifen -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary arteriosclerosis prophylaxis in men with a triple vessel disease. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Estrogen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular: Estrogen affects plasma lipids by increasing high-density lipoproteins (HDL) and triglyceride levels, while decreasing low-density lipoproteins (LDL) and total plasma cholesterol, and thereby reducing the risk of coronary artery disease in postmenopausal women. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Hormone Replacement Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Hormone Replacement Therapy in Women Older than 65
retrieved_docs.content: Discontinuing HRT can lead to a recurrence of vasomotor symptoms in about 50% of women, regardless of age or duration of use. No clear evidence suggests whether abrupt or gradual discontinuation is better for preventing these symptoms. Decisions about continuing HT should be tailored to each woman's symptoms and risk-benefit profile. ACOG advises against routinely stopping systemic estrogen at age 65, as some older women may still benefit from it for managing vasomotor symptoms. As with younger women, HRT decisions should be individualized based on each woman's specific needs and clinical situation. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Hormone Replacement Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Hormone Replacement Therapy in Women Older than 65
retrieved_docs.content: In May of 2024, an updated study published in Menopause, The Journal of the Menopause Society , looked at 13 different outcomes using 40 different hormonal therapies that were started or continued in women older than 65. They found that the use of estrogen-only HRT for those older than 65 was linked to significant reductions in mortality (19%) and risks for several conditions, including breast, lung, and colorectal cancer, congestive heart failure, VTE, atrial fibrillation, myocardial infarction, and dementia. The risk reduction seen with estradiol was more significant than that seen with conjugated estrogens. Marginal risk reductions were seen with endometrial and ovarian cancers, ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, and VTE with the use of estrogen plus natural progestin. The only risk reduction seen with estrogen plus progesterone was in congestive heart failure. The study suggested that low-dose, transdermal, or vaginal hormone therapies generally offer greater benefits and lower risks compared to medium or high doses or oral preparations, with estradiol showing more favorable outcomes than conjugated estrogen. The conclusion of this study, recommending risk mitigation with low dose and non-oral formulations as well as varying types of estrogen and progestogen, was in line with The Menopause Society's 2022 position statement. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In 2020 the American Heart Association developed a health prescription called "Life's Simple 7" designed to improve overall cardiovascular health and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease. These recommendations are outlined below.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1681
retrieved_docs.content: Dienogest should not be used in patients with coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي), as the medication is contraindicated in this condition due to the potential for adverse cardiovascular effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate is indicated as a possible treatment for menopausal disorders, while synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are each identified as potential therapies for alleviating menopausal symptoms. These estrogenic agents represent therapeutic options that can be employed to manage the various clinical manifestations associated with menopause, providing clinicians with multiple pharmacologic choices to address both the broader disorder and its specific symptomatic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 682
retrieved_docs.content: Dienogest is a progestogenic component of the combined oral contraceptive formulation dienogest/estradiol, and this combination product is classified within the group of progestogens and estrogens systemic contraceptives, sequential preparations. The use of dienogest, either alone or as part of the dienogest/estradiol preparation, is contraindicated in patients with مرض الشريان التاجي (coronary artery disease), reflecting the need to avoid this hormone therapy in individuals with established coronary vascular pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1179
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnadien derivatives, progestogens is a class of synthetic progestational agents that includes both dienogest and chlormadinone, making each of these compounds members of this chemical group. As members of the pregnadien‑derived progestogen class, dienogest and chlormadinone share structural characteristics that define their pharmacologic actions. Clinical use of dienogest requires particular caution because it is contraindicated in patients with coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي). Understanding the class membership of dienogest and chlormadinone, together with the specific cardiovascular contraindication for dienogest, assists clinicians in selecting appropriate hormonal therapies while minimizing the risk of adverse cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: and **Megestrol** – both Pregnadien‑derivatives that are included in progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives and are recognized as progestogens used for endocrine therapy. Progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives and the sequential progestogen‑estrogen preparations (e.g., dienogest/estradiol) are categorized as hormonal contraceptives for systemic use and fall within the broader family of emergency contraception (pohotovotní antikoncepce). The hierarchy therefore links structural derivative groups to the overarching category of progestogen sex hormones, situates individual agents within therapeutic contexts such as systemic hormonal contraception, endocrine therapy, and combination hormone products, and highlights safety considerations such as the contraindication of dienogest in coronary artery disease and the avoidance of medium‑chain triglycerides in hypertriglyceridemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2749
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate and synthetic conjugated estrogens, B are estrogenic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for managing the symptoms associated with menopause.

In lipid metabolism, medium‑chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients with elevated triglycerides, underscoring the need to select appropriate lipid‑modifying agents. Within the family of sex‑hormone modulators, the structural classes Estren‑derivatives, Pregnadien‑derivatives, and Pregnen‑4‑derivatives are all classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol and estrogeny are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for alleviating the symptoms associated with the menopausal transition. Both substances are forms of estrogen therapy and are considered in clinical management to improve quality of life for patients experiencing menopausal-related changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: Niacinamide is classified as an anti‑lipemic and anti‑atherosclerotic agent within the therapeutic class “Antilipaemica en geneesmiddelen tegen arteriosclerose” and is used to treat hypertriglyceridemia and may help prevent coronary artery disease. In contrast, medium‑chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients with elevated blood triglyceride levels. The ATC‑like hierarchy for sex hormones and genital‑system modulators places “SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM” at the top level, with subordinate categories that include “progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system,” “hormonal contraceptives for systemic use,” “gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants,” “other sex hormones and modulators of the genital system in ATC,” and “progestogens and estrogens in combination.” Within the progestogen subgroup, chemical families are organized as ISA members: “Estren derivatives, sex hormones and modulators of the genital system,” “Pregnadien derivatives,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: Antilipaemica en geneesmiddelen tegen arteriosclerose contains niacinamide as an active anti‑lipemic and anti‑arteriosclerotic component; niacinamide can be used to treat ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels) and to prevent مرض الشريان التاجي (coronary artery disease). Within the class of progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, several sub‑classes are defined. Estren‑derived progestogens include lynestrenol and norethindron; Pregnadien‑derived progestogens include chlormadinone, megestrol and dienogest; Pregnen‑4‑derived progestogens include medroxyprogesterone and progesterone. Lynestrenol, medroxyprogesterone and megestrol belong to the Progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives group, which is part of the broader Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs category (a type of hormones and related agents). Medroxyprogesterone is also classified as a Pregnen‑4 derivative, while chlormadinone and megestrol are classified as Pregnadien derivatives. Dienogest, a Pregnadien‑derived progestogen, is marketed in combination with estradiol (dienogest/estradiol) and is listed as a contraindication for patients with coronary artery disease. Progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives and progestogen‑and‑estrogen sequential preparations are subcategories of hormonal contraceptives for systemic use, which in turn are a form of pohotovostní antikoncepce (emergency contraception). The sequential preparations that combine progestogens and estrogens are specifically members of the dienogest / estradiol combination product group. Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients with ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم, reinforcing the therapeutic role of niacinamide in managing hypertriglyceridemia and reducing coronary artery disease risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 603
retrieved_docs.content: Dienogest is a progestogenic component of the combined hormonal preparation dienogest/estradiol, and this combination is classified as a member of the sequential progestogen‑estrogen systemic contraceptives. These sequential preparations are considered hormonal contraceptives for systemic use, which are further categorized under the broader group of emergency contraception (pohotovostní antikoncepce). In clinical practice, dienogest is contraindicated for patients with coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي), highlighting the need to avoid this agent in individuals with that cardiovascular condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: with chlormadinone are pregnadien‑derivative progestogens and are also listed among progestogens used for endocrine therapy. Clinically, dienogest is contraindicated in patients with coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي). In metabolic care, medium‑chain triglycerides are contraindicated for individuals with elevated triglyceride levels (ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم), whereas niacinamide may be employed to lower triglycerides and to help prevent coronary artery disease. This hierarchical framework clarifies how agents such as lynestrenol, norethindron, dienogest, tibolone, estrogen‑testosterone combinations, megestrol, and chlormadinone are positioned within the larger system of sex hormones and genital‑system modulators for therapeutic and contraceptive use.
